channel.title: NYT > U.S. > Politics
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: 5 Takeaways From Trump’s Address on Iran
item.description: President Trump did not define a clear path out of the conflict, which he estimated would end within three weeks.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater and Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Alex Brandon
item.media:description: President Trump giving a prime-time address from the White House on Wednesday.

item.title: Transcript: Trump’s Speech on Iran War
item.description: The president made his case for the U.S. attack, and said that the main objectives had been achieved.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: Judge Finds Border Officials Violated Previous Order on Warrantless Arrests
item.description: A federal judge in California ordered agents to thoroughly document any future stops in an area spanning 34 counties.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: A day laborer in Bakersfield, Calif., in January. A federal judge found on Wednesday that border officials appeared to have violated her earlier order when agents carried out an immigration sweep last year in a Home Depot parking lot in Sacramento.

item.title: U.S. Lifts Sanctions on Venezuela’s New Leader, Delcy Rodríguez
item.description: Removing sanctions would allow Delcy Rodríguez, the country’s acting leader, to conduct business with U.S. companies and potentially meet with President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Trump administration removed Delcy Rodríguez, the acting president of Venezuela, from its sanctions list on Wednesday.

item.title: D.H.S. Inspector General Inquiry Focuses in Part on Corey Lewandowski
item.description: The investigation comes as administration officials have fielded complaints about how Kristi Noem’s top adviser, Corey Lewandowski, dealt with companies seeking federal contracts.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz, Tyler Pager, Zolan Kanno-Youngs and Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kristi Noem, the former homeland security secretary, during a House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform hearing this year.

item.title: In Speech, Trump Is Expected to Call Iran War a Success
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump in the Oval Office on Tuesday. He has given contradictory statements about his goals and endgame for the war.

item.title: Trump Initially Laid Out Five Goals for the Iran War. Here’s Where They Stand.
item.description: The United States and Israel have done significant damage to Iran’s military capabilities. But Iran still fires missiles, has nuclear material and coordinates with militias in the region.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A new mural in Valiasr Square in Tehran on Wednesday. A large number of Iran’s ballistic missiles and launchers remain undamaged after a month of airstrikes by the United States and Israel.

item.title: Trump Has Discussed Firing Attorney General Pam Bondi
item.description: President Trump has not made a final decision, but he has floated the idea of replacing Ms. Bondi with Lee Zeldin, the E.P.A. administrator.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager
item.category: House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Among President Trump’s top complaints about Attorney General Pam Bondi is her handling of the Epstein files, which has become a political liability for the president among his supporters.

item.title: Trump-Backed Candidate in Louisiana Primary Also Praised DEI. Will It Hurt Her?
item.description: Representative Julia Letlow of Louisiana, running to challenge Senator Bill Cassidy, is facing conservative blowback over remarks from 2020.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Michael Johnson/The Advocate, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Representative Julia Letlow of Louisiana

item.title: Bondi Is Vulnerable as Republican Frustrations Over DOJ’s Epstein Files Missteps Grow
item.description: Ms. Bondi’s critics inside and outside the administration say she has made unforced errors that have turned the Justice Department’s handling of the Epstein files into a political crisis.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times

item.title: Iran Is Skeptical About Diplomacy, U.S. Intelligence Says
item.description: Any decision by Iran to keep fighting would complicate President Trump’s stated goal of trying to end the war within weeks.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong and Julian E. Barnes
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Multiple U.S. intelligence agencies have assessed that the Iranian government is not, for now, willing to engage in serious negotiations over ending the war.

item.title: Immigrant Families Are Cautiously Hopeful Over Supreme Court Birthright Citizenship Case
item.description: As the high court heard arguments on whether to limit birthright citizenship, many people pondered what the decision could mean for future generations.
item.dc:creator: Miriam Jordan and Zach Montague
item.media:credit: via the Andreote family
item.media:description: Maria and Francisco Andreote are Brazilian microbiologists conducting research at Penn State. Their daughter, Laura, 4, was born in the United States just before the birthright citizenship case landed in the Supreme Court.

item.title: The D.H.S. shutdown is the longest in history.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Wu

item.title: Pipe Bomb Defendant Says He May Adopt Debunked Account as Defense
item.description: Court papers show that Brian Cole Jr.’s lawyers might seek to blame a former Capitol Police officer whose name first surfaced in a right-wing media account and whom the F.B.I. briefly investigated.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Federal Bureau of Investigation
item.media:description: Surveillance footage from January 2021 showed the man authorities identified as Brian Cole Jr.

item.title: Senate and House Republicans Strike Deal to End DHS Shutdown
item.description: A bill to reopen the department, which the House G.O.P. rejected on Friday, could be approved as early as Thursday. It was a sharp turnaround by the lawmakers and President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: The resurrected plan would omit money for Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Border Patrol, the agencies carrying out President Trump’s immigration crackdown. Republicans said those agencies’ employees would continue to be paid out of funds that were pushed through Congress last year.

item.title: Five Takeaways From the Supreme Court’s Birthright Citizenship Case
item.description: The justices grappled with questions about domiciles and foundlings, avoided policy debates and mused about the sweep of possible rulings.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak and Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: The case argued on Wednesday focused on the constitutionality of an executive order seeking to end citizenship for babies born on U.S. soil to undocumented immigrants and temporary foreign visitors.

item.title: Pentagon Is Doubling Fleet of A-10 Attack Planes in Middle East
item.description: The slow-moving A-10 “Warthog” is a so-called close-air support plane that could be used to help U.S. ground forces seize territory near the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Brett Johnsen/NurPhoto, via Reuters
item.media:description: The presence of A-10s suggests that Iran’s strategic air defenses have been destroyed or greatly suppressed.

item.title: Ocasio-Cortez Says She Will Oppose All U.S. Military Aid to Israel
item.description: Representative Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez of New York said she would oppose U.S. military aid to Israel, including for defensive systems.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk, Benjamin Oreskes and Kellen Browning
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez has emerged as a leader in the Democratic Party and is considered a potential 2028 presidential candidate.

item.title: Homeland Security Dept. Asks Workers for Videos of Their Shutdown Hardships
item.description: The department’s public affairs office is seeking 30-second “selfie videos,” part of a push to pressure lawmakers to strike a deal.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo
item.media:credit: Damian Dovarganes/Associated Press
item.media:description: The Department of Homeland Security has been shut down for more than six weeks, with lawmakers deadlocked on a funding agreement.

item.title: Trump Administration Scales Back Plan to Dismantle Consumer Protection Bureau
item.description: A new filing asks a federal court to allow the White House to dismiss much of the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau’s remaining work force, but not close the bureau entirely.
item.dc:creator: Stacy Cowley
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Russell T. Vought, who is serving as the consumer bureau’s acting director, halted nearly all of the bureau’s work immediately after he arrived, and its duties went unperformed for most of last year.

item.title: ‘I Don’t Care About That’: Trump Says Iran’s Enriched Uranium Is Not a Concern
item.description: President Trump’s statement was the second time in 24 hours that he had declared that the nuclear problem with Iran had been solved, despite all evidence to the contrary.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: This morning, President Trump told Reuters that he didn’t care about Iran’s nuclear fuel because it is “so far underground.”

item.title: Didn’t the Supreme Court already hear this case?
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Protesters in favor of birthright citizenship outside the Supreme Court in May.

item.title: Under Trump’s Approach to Birthright Citizenship, ‘Foundlings’ Given Up for Adoption Could Be Stateless
item.description: Abandoned infants would have to produce evidence that at least one of their biological parents was a U.S. citizen or lawful permanent resident.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Todd Heisler/The New York Times
item.media:description: A 9-month-old girl who was born in the United States to Afghan immigrants having her photo taken for her U.S. passport.

item.title: Ending Birthright Citizenship Would Disproportionately Affect Asian Legal Immigrants
item.description: A new study found that an end to universal birthright citizenship would disproportionately affect babies born to Asian parents.
item.dc:creator: Miriam Jordan
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Demonstrators outside the Supreme Court on Wednesday.

item.title: Forget the 14th Amendment. A 1952 law also takes on birthright citizenship.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Protesters outside the Supreme Court on Wednesday.

item.title: Democrats Start to Hammer Vulnerable Republicans Over War in Iran in Ads
item.description: “Look at that gas pump,” a new ad from a liberal group says. It is targeting Representative Derrick Van Orden of Wisconsin over his support for the war effort.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Derrick Van Orden of Wisconsin, a retired Navy SEAL, is defending his seat in a competitive race this year.

item.title: Can Trump declare that his birthright rules apply only in the future?
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Eric Gay/Associated Press

item.title: Birthright Citizenship Plan Faces Data, Cost and Legal Hurdles
item.description: Experts warn that enforcing President Trump’s order to limit birthright citizenship would require building an expensive and fragmented verification system.
item.dc:creator: David W. Chen
item.category: Mexican-American Legal Defense and Education Fund
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: The country’s current system for recording and handling birth records is highly decentralized.

item.title: Placing U.S. Troops in Middle East Hotels May Violate Laws of War
item.description: U.S. commanders have kept many troops away from bases in the region to protect them from Iran’s ballistic missile attacks.
item.dc:creator: Thomas Gibbons-Neff
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Reuters
item.media:description: A drone attack hit a fuel tank near the Dubai International Airport amid the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran. U.S. commanders have relocated many of their troops because bases did not have adequate defenses to protect from Iranian ballistic missiles and drones.

item.title: The early exception to birthright citizenship for Native Americans and why it matters.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Library of Congress, via Getty Images
item.media:description: President Calvin Coolidge posing with a group of Native Americans at the White House, in 1927. President Coolidge signed the Indian Citizenship Act into law, granting citizenship to all Native Americans born in the United States.

item.title: Trump’s Birthright Citizenship Order Draws Lines Scholars Find Indefensible
item.description: Different treatment for mothers and fathers is at odds with a 2017 Supreme Court decision, and other ideas in the order are hard to understand.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump’s executive order curtailing birthright citizenship was one of the executive orders signed on his first day back in office.

item.title: Bruce Springsteen Brings Fiery Speeches and Songs to Minneapolis
item.description: The E Street Band opened its Land of Hope and Dreams tour on Tuesday night, where the musician asked the crowd to choose “unity over division and peace over war.”
item.dc:creator: Evelyn McDonnell
item.media:credit: Galen Fletcher for The New York Times
item.media:description: Bruce Springsteen and the E Street band onstage in Minneapolis on Tuesday night, where the group opened its Land of Hope and Dreams tour.

item.title: What Is Birth Tourism and How Common Is It?
item.description: The term refers to pregnant women who travel to the United States to give birth so that their baby can have American citizenship.
item.dc:creator: Amy Qin
item.media:credit: Eugene Hoshiko/Associated Press
item.media:description: Republican lawmakers have discussed birth tourism in the Northern Mariana Islands, a U.S. territory that is closer to China.

item.title: Trump Attends Supreme Court Oral Arguments, Then Leaves an Hour In
item.description: President Trump’s presence in the court, a presidential first, put him face to face with justices whom he has tried to bully and intimidate.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow, Zolan Kanno-Youngs and Miriam Jordan
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Chief Justice John G. Roberts Jr. and Justices Elena Kagan, Brett M. Kavanaugh and Amy Coney Barrett attended President Trump’s State of the Union address in February.

item.title: Lawyer Arguing at Supreme Court to Save Birthright Citizenship Is a Birthright Citizen
item.description: She has spent much of her career defending immigrants’ rights in America.
item.dc:creator: Amy Qin
item.media:credit: Steve Helber/Associated Press
item.media:description: Cecillia Wang, the American Civil Liberties Union’s national legal director, will be arguing for birthright citizenship before the Supreme Court.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle

item.title: Memphis House Primary Exemplifies Democrats’ Age Fights
item.description: A primary in Tennessee between Representative Steve Cohen, a white incumbent, and Justin Pearson, a Black state lawmaker, exemplifies a national push for a passing of the torch.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times; Brad J. Vest for The New York Times

item.title: Key Justices Appear Skeptical of Limiting Birthright Citizenship
item.description: President Trump appeared in court, watching as members of the court’s conservative majority raised questions about his efforts to limit birthright citizenship.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Supporters of birthright citizenship gathered outside the Supreme Court during oral arguments on Wednesday.

item.title: He Won Birthright Citizenship for All. His Own Family Never Knew.
item.description: Wong Kim Ark brought his case to the Supreme Court in 1898. But some of his descendants didn’t even know his name until about 15 years ago.
item.dc:creator: Amy Qin
item.media:credit: Minh Connors for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sandra and Norman Wong, the grandchildren of Wong Kim Ark, whose victory in the 1898 Supreme Court case affirmed automatic citizenship for nearly all children born in the United States.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs
